The score equation is a downward parabola with a clear peak value.
We find the vertex by taking the derivative of the function.
The derivative equals five minus point four times the number of sessions.
Setting this result to zero gives the optimal number of sessions.
Solving the simple algebraic equation yields a value of twelve point five.
Since sessions must be whole numbers, we test twelve and thirteen sessions.
Twelve sessions give a score of eighty point eight points total.
Thirteen sessions give a score of eighty point eight points total.
Both integer values provide the same maximum average batting score for the team.
The maximum average score is exactly eighty point eight points per game.

The win probability function uses the calculated score to predict future outcomes.
At five sessions, the score is fifty plus twenty five minus five.
This results in a score of seventy points for the five session case.
Plugging seventy into the logistic formula yields a probability of zero point eight eight.
At ten sessions, the score is fifty plus fifty minus twenty points.
This results in a score of eighty points for the ten session case.
Plugging eighty into the logistic formula yields a probability of zero point nine zero.
The change in win probability is the difference between these two values.
This difference equals zero point zero two or two percent increase in wins.
More practice leads to better scores which leads to more wins.
The team needs to balance effort with rest to maintain good health.

The final answer shows that increasing practice improves the team win rate.

The coach can now plan the schedule to maximize the team success.
